output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.3}}

Critique of the paragraph. The framing and the method/evidence don't match — several contributions are claimed but not actually delivered by what's described.

1. "Learning-based framework" is a mischaracterization. The method section describes a two-prompt pipeline over a *frozen* instruction-tuned LLM with no training, no fine-tuning, and no online updates. Nothing here learns. Either reframe the contribution as "prompt-based" / "LLM-driven" clarification, or add the actual training procedure. As written, the abstract-style framing is inconsistent with the implementation paragraph two sentences later.

2. Contribution (i) — "a learned ambiguity-detection module trained on a corpus of household instructions" — is not supported anywhere. The described detector is a self-scoring step on enumerated referents from a frozen LLM. There is no corpus, no training, no module. Drop this claim or describe the training data, objective, and held-out evaluation.

3. Contribution (ii) — "a clarification-generation policy that improves with user feedback over deployment" — is also unsupported. The evaluation is 40 static hand-authored commands; there is no deployment, no longitudinal user feedback loop, no policy update. Either remove this contribution or report the feedback-collection protocol and the learning curve that demonstrates improvement.

4. Contribution (iii) — task-failure reduction — is the only contribution actually evidenced (47% → 18%), but the evidence base is thin: one robot, one kitchen, 40 hand-authored commands from prior work (uncited), one baseline (always-take-argmax). No confidence intervals, no inter-rater agreement on the "user judges informative" metric, no ablation isolating which prompt does the work. Soften the "substantial improvements" claim or strengthen the evaluation.

5. The 35/40 ambiguity-detection and 31/40 informativeness numbers are reported without telling the reader how ground truth was established, who the judge was, or whether the judge was blind to condition. The 31/40 in particular looks like a single-rater subjective rating.

6. The concluding sentence ("position ClarifyBot as a step toward robot assistants that gracefully handle the ambiguity inherent in human language") is boilerplate and doesn't add information. Cut it or replace with a specific statement of scope and limitations (single environment, closed referent set, English, etc.).

7. Minor prose: "non-trivial probability mass" is vague — give the threshold. "Maximally informative referent" in paragraph 1 doesn't match "most discriminating attribute" in paragraph 2; pick one. The PDDL planner appears once and is never justified — either motivate the choice or remove the detail.

8. Citations: "drawn from prior work in instruction following" needs a reference; the instruction-tuned LLM is unnamed; the PDDL planner is unnamed. None of these are verifiable as stated.

Recommended minimum fix: rewrite the contributions list to match what was actually built (a prompt-pipeline system + an evaluation showing failure-rate reduction in one environment), drop the two unsupported "learned" claims, and add the missing methodological detail (judges, thresholds, model name, dataset source).

Uncertainty: I cannot verify whether a training corpus or feedback loop exists outside this excerpt — if they do, paragraphs 2–3 of the artifact should describe them and currently don't. Recommendations 1–3 assume the excerpt is representative of the full section.
